metadata.summary: Reduce embedding API spend via dimension reduction, batch requests, aggressive caching, two-stage retrieval with cheap models, and pre-ingestion deduplication.

content.title: Embedding Cost Optimization
content.summary: Five levers reduce embedding API cost without sacrificing retrieval quality: dimension reduction, batch API requests, aggressive caching, two-stage retrieval using a cheap filter model, and deduplication before ingestion. Applied together, typical savings exceed 80%.

why.p: At scale, embedding costs are dominated by ingestion volume and re-embedding on updates. text-embedding-3-large at $0.13/1M tokens costs $650/month for 10M 500-token documents. text-embedding-3-small at $0.02/1M tokens costs $100/month for the same workload. Dimension reduction from 3072 to 1024 preserves 95% retrieval quality while cutting storage costs 67%. Batching collapses 1000 API requests into 1. Caching eliminates repeat costs entirely. Each lever is independent and composable.

list.item: Embedding API cost is measurable on the monthly bill.
High-frequency re-ingestion with stable documents (incremental updates).
Two-stage retrieval patterns where first-pass recall can tolerate a cheaper model.
Small one-time ingestion (fewer than 10K documents) — optimization overhead exceeds savings.
Domain where quality is critical and cheaper models have not been benchmarked — do not substitute without measuring retrieval metrics.

section.name: cost-reference
section.p: Monthly embedding cost at 10M documents of 500 tokens each:

rule.statement: For text-embedding-3-large, use dimensions=1024 instead of the default 3072. This saves 67% storage and bandwidth with approximately 5% retrieval quality loss.
rule.rationale: OpenAI's Matryoshka Representation Learning allows reducing dimensions without full re-training. Benchmark on your retrieval task before committing — but 1024 dims consistently preserves 95%+ quality on MTEB benchmarks.

example.title: Dimension reduction via API parameter
example.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
# Instead of 3072 dimensions ($0.13/1M) # Use 1024 dimensions with minimal quality loss def get_embedding_reduced(text: str, dimensions: int = 1024) -> list[float]: response = client.embeddings.create( input=text, model="text-embedding-3-large", dimensions=dimensions, ) return response.data[0].embedding # Saves 67% storage, ~95% retrieval quality retained
example.note: Only text-embedding-3-large and text-embedding-3-small support the dimensions parameter. ada-002 does not.

rule.statement: Always batch embedding requests. OpenAI accepts up to 2048 texts per API call. Single-text loops waste 99% of available throughput and increase per-token cost due to per-request overhead.
rule.rationale: A single OpenAI embeddings call with 2048 texts is not meaningfully slower than a call with 1 text, but costs the same per-request fixed overhead. At 1000 texts, batching reduces API calls from 1000 to 1.

example.title: Batch embedding vs. per-text loop
example.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()
# Bad: 1000 API calls def embed_one_by_one(texts: list[str], model: str) -> list[list[float]]: results = [] for text in texts: resp = client.embeddings.create(input=text, model=model) results.append(resp.data[0].embedding) return results
# Good: 1 API call for up to 2048 texts def embed_batch(texts: list[str], model: str, batch_size: int = 2048) -> list[list[float]]: results = [] for i in range(0, len(texts), batch_size): batch = texts[i:i + batch_size] resp = client.embeddings.create(input=batch, model=model) results.extend([item.embedding for item in resp.data]) return results
example.note: For corpora larger than 2048, split into batches of 2048. The returned resp.data list preserves input order.

section.name: caching-lever
section.p: Cache hit rate of 80% = 80% cost reduction. See embedding-caching methodology for Redis implementation details.

example.title: Cache-first embed with fallback
example.code:
# Cache hit rate of 80% = 80% cost reduction cache = RedisEmbeddingCache()
def get_embedding_with_cache(text: str, model: str) -> list[float]: cached = cache.get(text, model) if cached: return cached embedding = client.embeddings.create(input=text, model=model).data[0].embedding cache.set(text, model, embedding) return embedding

section.name: two-stage-retrieval
section.p: Two-stage retrieval uses a fast, cheap embedding model for the first-pass filter (high recall, top-100), then a larger model or cross-encoder for reranking (high precision, top-10). This reduces large-model embedding calls by 10-50x.

example.title: Two-stage retrieval with cheap filter model
example.code:
# Two-stage retrieval: # Stage 1: Fast filter with small model — cheap, high recall quick_results = search_with_model(query, "all-MiniLM-L6-v2", top_k=100)
# Stage 2: Rerank with large model — expensive, high precision final_results = rerank_with_model(query, quick_results, "text-embedding-3-large", top_k=10)
example.note: Only 10 texts out of 100 candidates need the expensive embedding. 90% of large-model calls are eliminated.

rule.statement: Deduplicate texts by exact SHA256 hash before embedding to avoid paying for identical embeddings. Apply TF-IDF near-duplicate detection as an additional cost-reduction heuristic (not a semantic dedup guarantee).
rule.rationale: Deduplication before embedding via TF-IDF misses semantic duplicates (different wording, same meaning) — it is only a cost-reduction heuristic, not true semantic dedup. Exact dedup by hash is free and eliminates 100% of duplicate costs.

example.title: Exact hash deduplication before embedding
TF-IDF near-duplicate removal
example.note: TF-IDF deduplication is a cost heuristic. It will not catch paraphrases or same-meaning different-wording pairs. Use only as pre-embedding cost reduction, not as a semantic deduplication tool.

antipattern.description: Treating TF-IDF near-duplicate removal as semantic deduplication.
antipattern.reason: TF-IDF operates on surface token overlap. Two texts expressing the same fact with different vocabulary score low similarity. Semantic dedup requires embedding the texts first — the opposite of what you want for cost reduction.

checklist.check: Use dimensions=1024 for text-embedding-3-large (67% storage savings, ~5% quality loss).
Batch API requests — OpenAI accepts up to 2048 texts per call.
Implement Redis cache with model-namespaced keys and 30-day TTL.
Deduplicate by exact hash before batch ingestion.
Consider two-stage retrieval: cheap model for top-100 filter, expensive model for top-10 rerank.
Measure cache hit rate — target 70%+ for production RAG systems.
